title: Chapter 11
text: Chapter Eight L ara pulled the curtain open and stepped out of the tub, careful of her footing this time. Her skin was sensitive after the scrubbing she’d given it, tingling with the gentle touch of the air. She tugged the soft cloth from a rail on the wall — it was like a blanket, but too small — and wrapped it around herself to soak the water from her skin. Something moved at the corner of her eye . She jumped, afraid Ronin had entered, and nearly slipped again. But it was just her reflection in the foggy glass over the wash basin . After allowing her heart to slow down, she walked to the mirror and wiped off some of the condensation with the end of the cloth. The face looking back at her was both familiar and foreign. She’d seen her reflection in puddles and in some of the Market’s murky windows growing up, and she’d used one every night she worked at Kitty’s, but the last time had been months ago…and it was never so clear as this . The hot water had pinkened her otherwise pale skin. Her hair, still wet, was several shades darker than normal, framing her heart-shaped face in a tangled mess of curls. Gently arched brows rested over her wide, blue eyes. She parted her lips as she ran the tip of a finger down the bridge of her narrow nose. Her cheekbones and collarbone were more pronounced than she remembered, but it didn’t surprise her. Food hadn’t been plentiful lately . It served as a reminder of her lingering hunger . Lara narrowed her eyes, wiping at the dirt sprinkled over her cheeks and nose. When it didn’t go away, she leaned forward to examine it. They were little spots, light brown and delicate. Freckles. Tabitha had mentioned them, sometimes, but Lara had never seen them with her own eyes . She’d been told she was beautiful many times. Other women used it to their advantage, flaunted their beauty to feed themselves. That meant doing things Lara was unwilling to do; there’d been only one time, and it would never happen again . With those painful, frustrating memories threatening to rise, she pressed her hands onto the counter. Her palms settled on soft fabric. Glancing down, she found the shirt Ronin had left. She picked up the garment and held it out in front of her. It looked huge . She pulled it on, and, surely enough, the hem fell to her knees. But that didn’t matter; it was warm and dry, much better than her other options — put her sopping wraps back on or go out there naked . Gathering her clothing from the floor, she rinsed the fabric thoroughly in the tub and hung it over the curtain rod to dry. Why did a bot have all of this? Why did they need running water, beds, and toilets ? She assumed Ronin cleaned his outer skin, but that could be accomplished easily with a rag and a bucket of water, couldn’t it ? After she finished, she opened the door and peered into the hall. Light seeped beneath the closed door at the far end. That had to be Ronin’s room . Lara returned to her room and opened her bag. She spread the packages of food out atop the drawer box — dresser , she corrected herself — and hurriedly unfolded one of them. Plucking one of the potato wedges from within, she shoved the whole thing in her mouth. It was cold and a little chewy, but tasted heavenly . She devoured five more pieces of potato, several mouthfuls of carrot, and two slices of smoked meat before she forced herself to stop . “Oh my God,” she groaned, placing her hands on her stomach and closing her eyes. It hurt to be so full . And it felt amazing . She was tempted to eat more, but she’d likely puke. After so many years of scant meals, she couldn’t stand the thought of being so deliberately wasteful. Besides, Ronin had said the food was for tonight and the next morning. She rewrapped what remained and set the bundles aside . To keep herself from thinking about food, Lara removed the treasures from her bag. She stared at them for some time, considering the best way to arrange them in their new space. Finally, she tossed her pack aside and set to work, moving some of the items several times before their placement seemed right . She frowned when she lifted the picture. Water had stained it, damaging the color in a few spots. Just another thing on the verge of destruction. It had truly become part of her world, now. She propped it up against the wall; the faces were undamaged, at least, and anything that happened to the photo couldn’t change their joy . Lara returned to the hallway. Ronin’s light was still on, the door still shut. She walked to the stairs and went down. They could work out the details of their deal another time; she wanted to explore . The only furniture in the main room was a long table with a sturdy wooden chair. A few hand tools were scattered over the table’s surface, and there was a clamp of some kind attached to it on one side . The strange wood floor was cold as she turned and padded through the wide opening directly opposite the front door. After a bit of searching, she found the light switch. The lights flickered to life and revealed another strange room . To her right there was another table, this one taller, with four chairs positioned around it. A counter ran along the walls ahead and to her left, with another section standing free in the middle of the room. She counted at least fifteen or sixteen little doors beneath the counters and up on the walls; cupboards her memory whispered, though she only had the vaguest recollection of an old woman who’d used the word when Lara was very young . Curious, she walked to the counter and opened one of the doors. Empty apart from the shelf inside. She checked a few more, finding nothing. So much space, and nothing to show for it . How much could a bot really need, though ? There was another wash basin set in the counter, with a window behind it instead of a mirror. Lara pushed the handle on the spigot up. Water poured out. No delay, no pumping, just instant water . “Best not to waste it .” Lara looked over her shoulder. Ronin stood at the entrance of the room, his chest bare. Though it appeared to be one piece, portions of his skin were lighter and darker shades, reminding her of patchwork on a blanket. She shut off the water, turned to face him, and forced her eyes to remain on his. “I probably wasted a lot already .” “As much water as there is in Cheyenne, it’s still a limited resource .” “Why do you even need all this?” She spread her arms wide, indicating everything . “I don’t .” “But you have it. Clean, running water. Beds. Toilets . This place must have ten separate rooms !” “I have a place where I can clean my weapons and tend to my gear when I’m not in the Dust. Everything else is superfluous .” “What does super…what does that mean ?” “It means it’s unnecessary .” “Well, why didn’t you just say that? Either way, it’s all running . In good shape. And it’s necessary for us .” Lara swept her gaze around. There were more luxuries in this one room than she’d known throughout her entire life . “I bring in scrap and sell it. Got nothing to do with keeping this place running, except for providing some raw materials. There are other bots that handle the upkeep. They’re programmed to repair buildings, clean houses, cut grass, and keep appliances running. The Creators shaped them for those tasks, and they’ll perform them until they eventually break down .” “Is going into the Dust your purpose?” Her gaze dipped, moving over the line of his jaw to his thick neck . “Collecting trinkets yours ?” She met his eyes. “My purpose is to survive. I don’t go out of my way, those are just things that crossed my path .” “All paths in this word lead to dust. One way, or another .” “You’re evading!” The bots Lara had dealt with were always so blunt and direct. Why was this one so different ? “What does it matter? I’m surviving, same as you .” “Because I’m curious. You said there are bots made for only one purpose, and they know nothing else. But if you knew your purpose, you’d be doing it right now .” “How do you know I’m not fulfilling my programming right now ?” Lara narrowed her eyes. “And what programming would that be ?” “Evading your line of questioning .” She growled in frustration. “That is such a hu—” Something on his abdomen caught her attention, and she snapped her mouth shut. Everything about him screamed human male — his broad chest with its light brown nipples, his toned shoulders and arms, the sculpted ridges of his abdomen. Like the synth dancers in Kitty’s, he lacked a belly button, but there were three holes in the skin of his belly. Dull metal gleamed through them. “Are those…bullet holes ?” Ronin looked down. “I’m much better at dodging questions than I am other things, obviously .” “That happen in the Dust?” Lara stepped around the central counter and approached him, attention focused on the wounds — if they could even be called that . “ Yes .” She leaned down to examine the holes. The metal visible in each was intact but shiny, as though it had been freshly polished. Her brows fell; could bots feel pain? Had Ronin felt pain? Without thinking, she reached forward to touch the damaged skin . No touching . Lara stopped her hand before making contact and straightened. She sure as hell wasn’t going to give him an excuse to break that part of their deal. “What’s out there ?” The muscles — she knew he didn’t actually have any, but she didn’t have another name for the parts that moved his face — of his jaw bunched before he spoke. “The name says it all. Not much of anything but dirt, settled over the top of the old world. If you’re willing to dig, there are things of value hidden all over .” “How far have you gone ?” “How high can you count ?” Lara glared at him . “It’s a valid question .” “I’m not stupid .” Ronin’s eyes narrowed. “Never said you were .” “Damn near did .” “What does a number tell you if — ” “I can count,” she snapped. “I can’t read, but I can fucking count .” “To what? Millions? Trillions ?” “Just answer the damned question!” Her face heated. So what if she couldn’t count that high? He was probably just making up numbers to make her feel stupid . “I’ve gone just about everywhere that isn’t blocked by water. One million, two thousand, seven hundred and seventy-four miles. Or thereabouts .” It was a bigger number than she’d ever heard, which only increased its enormity to Lara. Still, something stirred inside of her, a dangerous feeling. Hope. If he’d truly gone so far, he’d seen other places. “It’s not all dust, right ?” “Most of it is,” Ronin replied, his expression softening. “Some places are better off than others. Cheyenne seems to have been spared the worst of it .” So this was as good as it got? “Why just this place ?” * * * “I didn’t say just this one. There are other towns where things are in order. Just like some were turned into craters where the rubble is so fine you can sift it through your fingers. Entire cities turned to dust .” Would it be better if he was unable to track the miles? If he couldn’t recall, with undiluted clarity, all the dead places he’d traversed? Somewhere, deep within his tattered data banks, memories of those places from before the Black Out awaited discovery . Uncovering those memories wasn’t likely to bring comfort . “Oh.” Lara glanced away, shoulders sagging. “Do you like what you do? Scavenging out in the Dust ?” Ronin’s processors whirred, analyzing her question and searching out the best answer. There wasn’t supposed to be like or dislike for him — only programming and its fulfillment . “It’s preferable to repairing buildings or mowing grass,” he finally replied . “So…you like being out there ?” How could he explain it to her? In the Dust, Ronin fought for his existence, earned it, and found some semblance of his purpose in the battle. It was not his programming — not entirely, or not exactly — but it was the closest he ever came . “I find moments of joy .” Her eyes — had the sky once been that shade of blue, or was it a fragment of corrupted data? — met his optics. “You actually feel joy ?” Why did she ask as though she didn’t believe it ? Why couldn’t Ronin formulate a satisfying answer ? “Perhaps…fulfillment is a better word.” It was no truer, though. Could someone be partially fulfilled ? “But you said enjoyment . Why would say that if it’s not what you meant ?” He tilted his head, scrutinizing Lara. She was difficult enough to decipher as it was. Her questions only complicated the puzzle . “Don’t humans often say things they don’t mean ?” “Yeah, but you’re not human .” The statement hung in the air between them. He couldn’t take offense in it; Ronin knew what he was, if not what he was for . Still, he suspected she’d meant it, at least in small part, as an insult . “Bots always say what they mean,” she continued when he didn’t respond, “and always do what they say .” “There are so many words with abstract, situational meanings. How are bots supposed to maintain a perfect track record when neither of our kinds fully understands them all the time?” Definitions only went so far to explain concepts like love, honor, and hatred. Beneath their simple explanations ran countless layers of emotion . “I may not know as many words as you,” Lara said, walking past him to sit on the edge of the table, “but I know the difference between fulfillment and enjoyment. And so do you .” In her new position, her shirt — his shirt — crept up, granting Ronin a glimpse of her lower thighs . That shirt touched me, and now it is touching her. Her nipples are brushing against it, that patch of copper hair is separated from my sight only by a thin bit of cloth … Ronin shifted his optics back to her face. How long had he stared? Seconds? By the set of her brows and the firmness of her mouth, it was long enough for her to notice. His processors could handle massive amounts of data at once, could simultaneously monitor his entire field of vision, his hearing, touch, and movement without any single thing diverting attention from another. Distraction didn’t happen for bots . How had this woman commanded all his attention? More specifically, how had her body done so? She should mean nothing to him . “So, since we’re talking about fulfillment…you want me to dance for you now ?” He wanted nothing more, and that was disconcerting . “No. Not tonight. Your end of the arrangement will begin tomorrow, when I return.” He didn’t trust himself to keep from breaking her conditions, not yet . “What do you mean, return ?” She lifted her posterior off the table and tugged the hem of the shirt down . “This place isn’t properly stocked to house a human. If this arrangement is going to last, I must keep my word. I must do what I said .” “So, you’re just leaving me here. Alone .” “Would you prefer I take you to the Market, where all the humans and bots will see the two of us together ?” The displeasure in her expression deepened. “Will I be safe here ?” “Yes. There’s a lock on the door .” She laughed. It was a strange sound to Ronin, akin to a word in an unfamiliar language; he understood only that it was devoid of humor. What would her laughter sound like when she was genuinely amused ? “I’m sure that’ll do wonders to keep bots out .” “Most of them , yes .” “But not all .” “You walked into this part of town with me, Lara Brooks .” “You can drop the Brooks . That’s not how humans refer to each other .” “I’m not human. Am I now expected to conform to your rules ?” “Sarcasm from a bot? Just when I thought I hit rock bottom …” “My point, unless you plan to go on another tangent, is that you saw what this area looks like when you came in. Do you really think I have anything in here that a bot can’t get elsewhere? There’s no reason for anyone to come .” “Guess this pit’s even lower than I thought .” Ronin tilted his head again, focusing his optics on her face. There was a subtle change in her expression; more light in her eyes, a slight bulge at her jaw. Small differences, but they didn’t strike him as good . “You’re offended,” he said . “I am not,” she replied, too quickly . “I’m afraid I don’t understand what I said to upset you .” “You’re afraid ? There you go again, using words you don’t mean .” Once again, her tone suggested an intent to insult him. He reviewed their conversation, sifting through every word, hoping to discover what had provoked this reaction from Lara . “Explain it to me .” “I’m not some meatbag, bot-banging whore!” She pushed away from the table and approached him, bare feet slapping on the floor . “No touching,” he said carefully. “That was the agreement .” “As if I’d willingly touch you!” She stalked past him, toward the stairs . Information blazed across his processors, threatening to blow a circuit. There had to be a reason. He’d said something wrong, at some point. But what? Had he simply underestimated the complexity of humans and their emotions ? And yet…her anger had sparked something inside him. Something hot, akin to the impatience he’d experienced with the gearheads earlier. That wasn’t entirely correct, but he could get no closer in his understanding of it . He walked after her, his boots far louder on the floorboards than her feet, and caught up as she reached the bottom step . “You do not get to say things like that to me and then walk away,” he said . Lara paused with a hand on the railing and twisted to look at him, her expression hard. “I’m not walking . I’m storming .” As though to prove it, she continued up the stairs, stomping her feet. “See? I said what I meant, and I’m doing it !” Ronin followed, despite a brief risk assessment warning a chance of further damage in the process. Humans were volatile; Lara took the term to a new level. An electric tingle arced across his palm. An impulse, perhaps result of damaged coding somewhere deep inside, to reach out and grab her arm. To force her to stop and talk . That would guarantee a poor ending to the confrontation . The heat in his mind faded; the situation was beyond his ability to comprehend. It was almost amusing. This had to be born of a simple miscommunication. She’d interpreted something he said in an unintended fashion — he’d failed to say what he meant — and it escalated into this . He halted at the top of the stairs as a phrase flitted across his processors. What the fuck ? Though he’d never heard Lara say those words together, they were in her voice, and fit the situation perfectly . For the first time in his remembrance, Ronin laughed. It was short, similar to the sound most humans made after being punched in the gut, but it was a laugh . Lara stood in the doorway to her room, already at the end of the hallway. She stared at Ronin as he approached, her jaw agape . “Don’t you dare fucking laugh at me !” She slammed the door shut. It rattled in its frame, the vibrations running through the nearby walls, and Ronin registered displaced air flow over his bared skin. Lara fumbled on the other side. After a muffled curse, the lock clicked into place . Ronin stood in place for a time, optics focused on the door. There was no diffusing the situation; it had already exploded. He didn’t desire an adversarial relationship with Lara. All he wanted — all he told himself he wanted — was to see her dance . He couldn’t leave things like this . He tapped on the door . “ Go . Away .” “Open the door,” he said, moderating his tone to something gentle . “ No .” She’d been right in thinking that doors — especially the relatively flimsy ones inside the house — weren’t an obstacle to bots. But what would he accomplish by breaking in? He was not her master or her owner, not even her keeper. He’d offered her food, comfort, and security . “I’m sorry I upset you , Lara .” His internal clock tracked every passing microsecond, and soon, time seemed to lose meaning. She didn’t answer. Once, there was a faint rustle of cloth that might have been her moving, but it was gone too quickly to be sure . After five minutes and thirty-three seconds, Ronin turned around and walked to his room. He had equipment to care for; weapons to clean, tools to inspect, clothing to mend . Perhaps some rest would cool Lara . Perhaps he needed to be more mindful of what he said to her . I ’m sorry I upset you , Lara . She stared at the door, speechless, her anger vanished. When she’d slammed it shut, she knew he was going to kick it in and teach her a lesson. Determined not to show her fear, she was ready to fight tooth and nail. To the death . Ronin had asked her to open the door. And apologized to her after she refused . What the fuck ? Never in her twenty-three years had she heard of a bot apologizing to a human . His footsteps retreated down the hall, and his door opened and closed softly. She’d come into this agreement assuming she knew all about bots and their nature. Accepting that, even if Ronin didn’t harm her, she’d be nothing more than a pet to him, a curiosity for his amusement. She could have accepted that if it helped her find Tabitha . But Ronin was different. He was unpredictable, surprisingly deep, even kind of funny in an infuriating way. No matter how hard she tried, she couldn’t quite figure him out . Remorse tightened her chest. Though he was good at evading her questions, he hadn’t been lying; he didn’t understand what triggered her outburst. How could he? Even if he displayed signs of other emotions, he had nothing to be insecure about. He couldn’t know how insignificant his words had made her feel . Do you really think I have anything in here that a bot can’t get elsewhere ? Before her thoughts took another dark turn, she forced herself to the bed. Her day had started early, and her encounter with Devon as she left town had only set the tone for the rest of it — the rainstorm and loss of the ring, the ups-and-downs with Ronin … Their last fight had left her physically and mentally exhausted . She pulled back the blanket and tested the bed’s softness with a hand. Would she even be able to sleep on this cushy thing, in a strange place, knowing she was on the wrong side of the wall ? Glancing to the light switch near the door, she paused, taking her lower lip between her teeth . Too tired to go back and turn it off , she told herself. Guess I’m too tired to call my own bullshit , too . She lay down and pulled the blanket up to her chin. The bed gave way beneath her, cradling her body, enveloping her in softness and warmth. She didn’t remember closing her eyes before weariness claimed her .
